\section{Batch Claims}
% =============================
\begin{batchclaim}[id=darwiche-and-c-clause-cnf-lower-bound, op=AND_C, status=no-quasi]
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ in
\[
\{
  \langref{cSDD},
  \langref{d-SDNNF},
  \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T},
  \langref{DNF},
  \langref{DNNF},
  \langref{FBDD},
  \langref{nFBDD},
  \langref{nOBDD},
  \langref{SDD},
  \langfam{SDD}{T},
  \langref{SDNNF},
  \langfam{SDNNF}{T},
  \langref{uFBDD},
  \langref{uOBDD},
  \langref{OBDD},
  \langref{dec-DNNF},
  \langref{dec-SDNNF},
  \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T},
  \langfam{cSDD}{T},
  \langfam{nOBDD}{<},
  \langfam{uOBDD}{<}
\},
\]
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Conjunction not in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,TCZ_Forthcoming}.
\end{batchclaim}
\begin{claimdescription}
In the language $\mathcal{L}$, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If $\mathcal{L}$ supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into $\mathcal{L}$ by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nedgeref{CNF}{\mathcal{L}} \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}.
\end{claimdescription}

\begin{batchclaim}[id=darwiche-or-c-term-dnf-lower-bound, op=OR_C, status=no-quasi]
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ in
\[
\{
  \langref{cSDD},
  \langref{d-SDNNF},
  \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T},
  \langref{FBDD},
  \langfam{OBDD}{<},
  \langfam{SDD}{T},
  \langref{uOBDD},
  \langref{OBDD},
  \langref{dec-DNNF},
  \langref{dec-SDNNF},
  \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T},
  \langfam{cSDD}{T},
  \langfam{uOBDD}{<}
\},
\]
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Disjunction not in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002,TCZ_Forthcoming}.
\end{batchclaim}
\begin{claimdescription}
In the language $\mathcal{L}$, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If $\mathcal{L}$ supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into $\mathcal{L}$ by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nedgeref{DNF}{\mathcal{L}} \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}.
\end{claimdescription}

\begin{batchclaim}[id=darwiche-fo-cnf-consistency-lower-bound, op=FO, status=no-poly-unknown-quasi, assumption=P \neq NP]
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ in
\[
\{
  \langref{CNF},
  \langref{NNF}
\},
\]
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Forgetting not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) assuming P \neq NP \citet{Darwiche_2002}.
\end{batchclaim}
\begin{claimdescription}
If $\mathcal{L}$ supported FO, then the consistency of any CNF sentence $\Sigma$ could be tested in polynomial time: represent $\Sigma$ in $\mathcal{L}$, compute $\exists\text{Vars}(\Sigma).\Sigma$, and check the resulting constant sentence. This would decide CNF consistency in polynomial time, implying P = NP (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

\begin{batchclaim}[id=darwiche-fo-dnf-selector-lower-bound, op=FO, status=no-poly-unknown-quasi]
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ in
\[
\{
  \langref{OBDD},
  \langfam{OBDD}{<},
  \langfam{SDD}{T},
  \langfam{cSDD}{T}
\},
\]
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Forgetting not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) \citet{Darwiche_2002,VanDenBroeck_2015}.
\end{batchclaim}
\begin{claimdescription}
A polynomial-time forgetting procedure for $\mathcal{L}$ would turn every \langref{DNF} sentence into an equivalent polynomial-size $\mathcal{L}$ representation after introducing fresh selectors and forgetting them. This contradicts \nedgeref{DNF}{\mathcal{L}} \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}.
\end{claimdescription}


% =============================
\section{A. Darwiche and P. Marquis, "A Knowledge Compilation Map", Journal of Artificial...}
% Reference ID: Darwiche_2002
% =============================
% lang=lang_89649e36, op=AND_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{CNF} supports Conjunction in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conjunction of CNF sentences is trivially another CNF sentence: simply take the union of all clauses (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_89649e36, op=OR_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{CNF} supports Bounded Disjunction in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
The disjunction of two CNF sentences $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2$ can be computed by taking all cross products of one clause from $\alpha_1$ and one from $\alpha_2$, removing redundant literals and valid clauses. The result is a CNF of polynomial size (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_89649e36, op=OR_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{CNF} supports Disjunction not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
It is straightforward to convert any term into an equivalent CNF formula in polynomial time. Since specific DNF formulas cannot be turned into equivalent CNF formulas in polynomial space (DNF $\not\leq$ CNF), CNF does not satisfy $\lor$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_89649e36, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{CNF} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
CD is trivially satisfied: replacing literals of the conditioning term by Boolean constants in a CNF and removing valid clauses preserves the CNF property (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_6c130090, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{d-DNNF} supports Bounded Conjunction not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) assuming P \neq NP \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Since OBDD $\subseteq$ d-DNNF and d-DNNF satisfies CO, the NP-completeness of consistency checking for two OBDDs (which may possess different underlying orderings) (Lemma 8.14, \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}) implies d-DNNF does not satisfy $\land$BC assuming P \neq NP (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_6c130090, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{d-DNNF} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning preserves both decomposability and determinism. If $\alpha \land \beta \models \bot$ then $(\alpha|\gamma) \land (\beta|\gamma) \models \bot$ for any consistent term $\gamma$, since $((\alpha \land \beta)|\gamma) \land \gamma \models \bot$ and $\gamma$ is consistent (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_6c130090, op=SFO
\begin{claim}
\langref{d-DNNF} supports Singleton Forgetting not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) assuming P \neq NP \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
If d-DNNF satisfies SFO, then it satisfies $\lor$BC (since $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2 \equiv \exists x . ((x \land \alpha_1) \lor (\neg x \land \alpha_2))$ for fresh $x$). But d-DNNF does not satisfy $\lor$BC assuming P \neq NP (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_4c204bf3, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{DNF} supports Bounded Conjunction in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
The conjunction of two DNF sentences $\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2$ can be computed by taking all cross products of one term from $\alpha_1$ and one from $\alpha_2$, removing redundant literals and inconsistent terms. The result is a DNF of polynomial size (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_4c204bf3, op=OR_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{DNF} supports Disjunction in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Disjunction of DNF sentences is trivially another DNF sentence: simply take the union of all terms (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_3bebcab7, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{DNNF} supports Bounded Conjunction not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) assuming P \neq NP \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Checking consistency of two OBDDs (which may possess different underlying variable orderings) is NP-complete (Lemma 8.14, \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}). Since OBDD $\subseteq$ DNNF and DNNF satisfies CO, if DNNF satisfied $\land$BC then consistency of two OBDDs could be tested in polytime, implying P = NP (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_3bebcab7, op=OR_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{DNNF} supports Disjunction in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Disjunction trivially preserves decomposability since decomposability only constrains $\land$-nodes. (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_3bebcab7, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{DNNF} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning preserves decomposability: if $\alpha$ and $\beta$ do not share variables, then $\alpha|\gamma$ and $\beta|\gamma$ do not share variables either, since $\text{Vars}(\alpha|\gamma) \subseteq \text{Vars}(\alpha)$ (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_684b1ca7, op=NOT_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{FBDD} supports Negation in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Negation of an FBDD is achieved by switching the labels of the 0-sink and 1-sink. The decision property and read-once property are preserved (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_684b1ca7, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{FBDD} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning on FBDD is the restrict operation: replace each node labeled by a variable in the conditioning term with one of its children. This preserves the free (read-once) property (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_684b1ca7, op=SFO
\begin{claim}
\langref{FBDD} supports Singleton Forgetting not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) assuming P \neq NP \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
If FBDD satisfies SFO, then it satisfies $\lor$BC (since disjunction can be encoded via SFO with a fresh variable). But FBDD does not satisfy $\lor$BC assuming P \neq NP (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_6ae90adc, op=NOT_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{IP} supports Negation not in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Dual of PI: $\Sigma_n = \bigvee_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_{2i} \land x_{2i+1})$ is in IP form but has $2^n$ prime implicates. Its negation has $2^n$ prime implicants. Hence IP cannot satisfy $\neg$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_6ae90adc, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{IP} supports Bounded Conjunction in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
$\text{IP}(\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2) = \max(\{\beta_1 \land \beta_2 \mid \beta_1 \in \text{IP}(\alpha_1), \beta_2 \in \text{IP}(\alpha_2)\}, \models)$ (dual of Proposition 40, Marquis 2000). The maximization removes subsuming terms, yielding an IP form in polytime (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_6ae90adc, op=AND_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{IP} supports Conjunction not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
It is straightforward to convert a clause into an equivalent IP formula in polynomial time. Since specific CNF formulas cannot be turned into equivalent IP formulas in polynomial space (CNF $\not\leq$ IP), IP does not satisfy $\land$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_6ae90adc, op=OR_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{IP} supports Bounded Disjunction not in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Combining prime implicants via disjunction requires distributing and re-minimizing terms, which can lead to an exponential explosion of new prime implicants. Let $\alpha_1 = \bigwedge_{i=1}^k p_i$ (one prime implicant) and $\alpha_2 = \bigvee_{i=1}^k \bigvee_{j=1}^m (\neg p_i \land q_{i,j})$ ($mk$ prime implicants). Then $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2$ has $(m+1)^k + mk$ prime implicants, an exponential blowup \citet{Chandra_1978} (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_6ae90adc, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{IP} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Given IP formula $\Sigma = \bigvee \gamma_i$, conditioning on $\gamma$ gives $\bigvee (\gamma_i|\gamma)$. Keeping only the logically weakest surviving terms yields a prime implicants formula equivalent to $\Sigma|\gamma$. This requires $O(n^2)$ entailment tests among terms, each polytime (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_6ae90adc, op=SFO
\begin{claim}
\langref{IP} supports Singleton Forgetting not in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
The number of prime implicants of $\exists x.\Sigma$ can be exponentially greater than the number of prime implicants of $\Sigma$ \citet{Chandra_1978}. A specific construction with $mk+1$ prime implicants produces $(m+1)^k + mk$ prime implicants after forgetting one variable (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_e02902d0, op=NOT_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{MODS} supports Negation not in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
$\Sigma = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n x_i$ has only one model over $\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}$ but its negation $\neg\Sigma$ has $2^n - 1$ models. Hence MODS cannot satisfy $\neg$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_e02902d0, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{MODS} supports Bounded Conjunction in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
The bounded conjunction of two MODS formulas (over the same variables) is simply taking the intersection of their model sets. If the variables differ, it is the intersection of their cylindrical extensions. The result is a MODS sentence in polytime (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_e02902d0, op=AND_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{MODS} supports Conjunction not in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Let $\Sigma_i = (x_{i,1} \lor x_{i,2})$ for $i = 1,\ldots,n$. Each $\Sigma_i$ has 3 models. But $\Sigma = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n \Sigma_i$ has $3^n$ models, so no polynomial-size MODS representation exists (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_e02902d0, op=OR_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{MODS} supports Bounded Disjunction not in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Let $\alpha_1 = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n x_i$ (1 model) and $\alpha_2 = y$ (1 model). Then $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2$ has $2^n + 1$ models over $\text{Vars}(\alpha_1) \cup \text{Vars}(\alpha_2)$. Since this already uses the disjunction of just two MODS sentences, MODS does not satisfy $\lor$BC (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_e02902d0, op=FO
\begin{claim}
\langref{MODS} supports Forgetting in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Given a MODS formula $\Sigma$ and a set $X$, the MODS representation of $\exists X.\Sigma$ is obtained by removing every leaf node labeled by a literal $x$ or $\neg x$ with $x \in X$ (Propositions 18 and 20, Lang et al. 2000). See also the polytime FO operation on DNNF from \citet{Darwiche_2001a} (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_5bf00851, op=NOT_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{NNF} supports Negation in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
NNF trivially satisfies $\neg$C since any propositional sentence can be converted to NNF by pushing negations to literals and this preserves the NNF property (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_5bf00851, op=AND_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{NNF} supports Conjunction in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Taking the conjunction of NNF sentences $\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2 \land \ldots$ trivially produces another NNF sentence, since NNF places no restrictions on $\land$-nodes (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_5bf00851, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{NNF} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
CD is trivially satisfied by NNF: replacing literals of the conditioning term by Boolean constants preserves the NNF property (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_b9d72a7c, op=NOT_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{OBDD} supports Negation in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Negation of an OBDD is achieved by switching the labels of the 0-sink and 1-sink. The ordered variable property is preserved since the single ordering is not affected (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_b9d72a7c, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{OBDD} supports Bounded Conjunction not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) assuming P \neq NP \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Checking consistency of two OBDDs (which may possess different underlying orderings) is NP-complete (Lemma 8.14, \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}). Since OBDD satisfies CO, if OBDD satisfied $\land$BC consistency could be tested in polytime, implying P = NP (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_b9d72a7c, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{OBDD} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Since OBDD $\subseteq$ FBDD and FBDD satisfies CD (the restrict operation), OBDD also satisfies CD. The variable ordering is preserved under conditioning (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_b9d72a7c, op=SFO
\begin{claim}
\langref{OBDD} supports Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Since only one OBDD sentence is considered in the transformation, and OBDD$_<$ satisfies SFO, OBDD also satisfies SFO (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_27fffab2, op=NOT_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{PI} supports Negation not in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
The formula $\Sigma_n = \bigwedge_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_{2i} \lor x_{2i+1})$ is in PI form but has $2^n$ prime implicants. Its negation has $2^n$ prime implicates. Hence PI cannot satisfy $\neg$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_27fffab2, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{PI} supports Bounded Conjunction not in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Combining prime implicates via conjunction requires distributing and re-minimizing clauses, which can lead to an exponential explosion of new prime implicates. Let $\alpha_1 = \bigvee_{i=1}^k p_i$ (one prime implicate) and $\alpha_2 = \bigwedge_{i=1}^k \bigwedge_{j=1}^m (\neg p_i \lor q_{i,j})$ ($mk$ prime implicates). Then $\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2$ has $(m+1)^k + mk$ prime implicates, an exponential blowup \citet{Chandra_1978} (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_27fffab2, op=OR_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{PI} supports Bounded Disjunction in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
$\text{PI}(\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2) = \min(\{\beta_1 \lor \beta_2 \mid \beta_1 \in \text{PI}(\alpha_1), \beta_2 \in \text{PI}(\alpha_2)\}, \models)$ (Proposition 40, Marquis 2000). The minimization removes subsumed clauses, yielding a PI form in polytime (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_27fffab2, op=OR_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{PI} supports Disjunction not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
It is straightforward to convert any term into an equivalent PI formula in polynomial time. Since specific DNF formulas cannot be turned into equivalent PI formulas in polynomial space (DNF $\not\leq$ PI), PI does not satisfy $\lor$C (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_27fffab2, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{PI} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
The prime implicates of $\Sigma \land \gamma$ can be computed in polytime when $\Sigma$ is in PI form and $\gamma$ is a term (Proposition 36, Marquis 2000). Since PI satisfies FO, the prime implicates of $\Sigma|\gamma \equiv \exists\text{Vars}(\gamma).(\Sigma \land \gamma)$ follow in polytime (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_27fffab2, op=FO
\begin{claim}
\langref{PI} supports Forgetting in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2002}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
The prime implicates of $\exists X.\Sigma$ are exactly the prime implicates of $\Sigma$ that do not contain any variable from $X$ (Proposition 55, Marquis 2000). Hence FO for PI simply filters out clauses containing forgotten variables (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}


% =============================
\section{K. Pipatsrisawat and A. Darwiche, "New Compilation Languages Based on Structured...}
% Reference ID: Pipatsrisawat_2008
% =============================
% lang=lang_ea9b5299, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{d-SDNNF} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning preserves both structured decomposability and determinism. The vtree structure is maintained since $\text{Vars}(\Sigma|\gamma) \subseteq \text{Vars}(\Sigma)$, and mutual exclusivity of $\lor$-children is preserved under conditioning (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_ea9b5299, op=FO
\begin{claim}
\langref{d-SDNNF} supports Forgetting not in quasi-polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
If \langref{d-SDNNF} satisfied FO, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled to an equivalent \langref{d-SDNNF} of polynomial size (using fresh selector variables and applying FO). Since specific \langref{DNF}s require exponential-size \langref{d-SDNNF} representations (unconditionally), this is impossible. This unconditional result applies to the fixed-vtree case (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_91f812d0, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} supports Bounded Conjunction in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Bounded conjunction of two \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} formulas can be performed in polytime via the Apply algorithm, preserving both determinism and structured decomposability (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_91f812d0, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning preserves both decomposability and determinism, and it preserves the fixed vtree because restricting by a term only removes variables or replaces literals by constants. Hence the conditioned formula remains in \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_91f812d0, op=FO
\begin{claim}
\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} supports Forgetting not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
For a linear vtree $T$ compatible with an order $<$, every \langfam{OBDD}{<} formula is a \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} formula, and every such \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} formula can be converted back to \langfam{OBDD}{<} in polynomial time. If \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} supported FO in polynomial time, then \langfam{OBDD}{<} would support FO in polynomial time, contradicting the known \langfam{OBDD}{<} lower bound (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_b13b0d78, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{SDNNF} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning preserves structured decomposability: replacing conditioned literals by constants and simplifying can only remove variables from subformulas, so every decomposable split continues to respect the original vtree. Therefore the result remains an \langref{SDNNF} formula of polynomial size (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_b13b0d78, op=FO
\begin{claim}
\langref{SDNNF} supports Forgetting in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Forgetting preserves structured decomposability. Replacing all occurrences of literal $x$ and $\neg x$ with $\top$ in an \langref{SDNNF} preserves the vtree structure, since the decomposition at $\land$-nodes remains valid after variable removal. Inherited from \langref{DNNF} (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_3c803ba1, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langfam{SDNNF}{T} supports Bounded Conjunction in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Bounded conjunction of two \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formulas can be performed in polytime via the Apply algorithm. The fixed vtree governs variable partitioning at $\land$-nodes, enabling an efficient recursive traversal (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_3c803ba1, op=OR_C
\begin{claim}
\langfam{SDNNF}{T} supports Disjunction in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Disjunction of \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formulas preserves structured decomposability, since decomposability only constrains $\land$-nodes and a top-level $\lor$-node introduces no new $\land$-node constraints (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_3c803ba1, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langfam{SDNNF}{T} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning preserves decomposability with respect to the same fixed vtree: after replacing conditioned literals by constants, each remaining subformula mentions a subset of its previous variables, so every $\land$-node still decomposes according to the original vtree split (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_3c803ba1, op=FO
\begin{claim}
\langfam{SDNNF}{T} supports Forgetting in polynomial time \citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Darwiche's DNNF projection operation for forgetting variables preserves decomposability with respect to any fixed vtree. Applying that operation to a \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formula therefore yields an equivalent \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formula in polynomial time (\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2008}, Proposition 4).
\end{claimdescription}


% =============================
\section{Tractable Circuit Zoo, "Tractable Circuit Zoo", forthcoming.}
% Reference ID: TCZ_Forthcoming
% =============================
% lang=lang_1df07cc3, op=OR_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{nFBDD} supports Disjunction in polynomial time \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Disjunction of \langref{nFBDD} formulas is obtained by adding a top-level nondeterministic $\lor$-node whose children are the input formulas. This preserves the free read-once property.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_1df07cc3, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{nFBDD} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning is the restrict operation: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term, or equivalently prune inconsistent edges and simplify. This preserves the free read-once property.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_1df07cc3, op=FO
\begin{claim}
\langref{nFBDD} supports Forgetting in polynomial time \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
To forget a variable, replace each decision node labeled by that variable with a nondeterministic $\lor$-node over its two children. Repeating this for all forgotten variables preserves the free read-once property and computes existential quantification.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_d24efe0e, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{nOBDD} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning is the restrict operation: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term, or equivalently prune inconsistent edges and simplify. This preserves the ordered decision-diagram property.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_d24efe0e, op=FO
\begin{claim}
\langref{nOBDD} supports Forgetting in polynomial time \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
To forget a variable, replace each decision node labeled by that variable with a nondeterministic $\lor$-node over its two children. Repeating this for all forgotten variables preserves the ordered decision-diagram property and computes existential quantification.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_e827cf31, op=OR_C
\begin{claim}
\langfam{nOBDD}{<} supports Disjunction in polynomial time \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Disjunction of \langfam{nOBDD}{<} formulas is obtained by adding a top-level nondeterministic $\lor$-node whose children are the input formulas. Since all inputs respect the same fixed order $<$, the result is still a \langfam{nOBDD}{<} formula.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_e827cf31, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langfam{nOBDD}{<} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning is the restrict operation: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term, or equivalently prune inconsistent edges and simplify. The fixed order $<$ is preserved.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_e827cf31, op=FO
\begin{claim}
\langfam{nOBDD}{<} supports Forgetting in polynomial time \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
To forget a variable, replace each decision node labeled by that variable with a nondeterministic $\lor$-node over its two children. Repeating this for all forgotten variables preserves the fixed order $<$ and computes existential quantification.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_4e62a038, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{uFBDD} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning is the restrict operation: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term, or equivalently prune inconsistent edges and simplify. This preserves the free read-once property and cannot create two accepting paths for a remaining assignment.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_c2df8c2b, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{uOBDD} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming,Wegener_2000}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning is the restrict operation: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term, or equivalently prune inconsistent edges and simplify. This preserves the ordered decision-diagram property and cannot create two accepting paths for a remaining assignment. For a similar closure property for parity nondeterminism, see Theorem~10.5.1 of \citet{Wegener_2000}.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_43a33aec, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langfam{uOBDD}{<} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning is the restrict operation: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term, or equivalently prune inconsistent edges and simplify. The fixed order $<$ is preserved, and the restriction cannot create two accepting paths for a remaining assignment.
\end{claimdescription}


% =============================
\section{A. Darwiche, "SDD: a new canonical representation of propositional knowledge bas...}
% Reference ID: Darwiche_2011
% =============================
% lang=lang_1afefbe2, op=NOT_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{SDD} supports Negation in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
An SDD can be negated in polytime by applying exclusive-or with $\top$ using the Apply algorithm \citet{Darwiche_2011}.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_1afefbe2, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langref{SDD} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning an SDD $f$ on a literal $l$ is equivalent to computing $f \land l$. Since any literal is a trivially small SDD and SDD supports $\land$BC in polytime via Apply, conditioning is polytime \citet{Darwiche_2011}.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_9c84a267, op=NOT_C
\begin{claim}
\langfam{SDD}{T} supports Negation in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Negation of an \langfam{SDD}{T} can be computed in polytime by applying exclusive-or with $\top$ using Apply. Since $\top$ trivially respects any fixed vtree $T$, the result remains an \langfam{SDD}{T} over the same vtree \citet{Darwiche_2011}.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_9c84a267, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langfam{SDD}{T} supports Bounded Conjunction in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
The Apply algorithm combines two \langfam{SDD}{T} sentences using any Boolean operator in polytime. In particular, two \langfam{SDD}{T} formulas can be conjoined in polytime via Apply \citet{Darwiche_2011}.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_9c84a267, op=OR_BC
\begin{claim}
\langfam{SDD}{T} supports Bounded Disjunction in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
The Apply algorithm combines two \langfam{SDD}{T} sentences using any Boolean operator in polytime. In particular, two \langfam{SDD}{T} formulas can be disjoined in polytime via Apply \citet{Darwiche_2011}.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_9c84a267, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langfam{SDD}{T} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2011}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning an \langfam{SDD}{T} sentence $f$ on a literal $l$ is equivalent to computing $f \land l$. Since a literal is a trivially small \langfam{SDD}{T} respecting the same fixed vtree, and \langfam{SDD}{T} supports $\land$BC in polytime via Apply, conditioning is polynomial-time as well \citet{Darwiche_2011}.
\end{claimdescription}


% =============================
\section{C. Meinel and T. Theobald, "Algorithms and Data Structures in VLSI Design: OBDD ...}
% Reference ID: Meinel_Theobald_1998
% =============================
% lang=lang_1df07cc3, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{nFBDD} supports Bounded Conjunction not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) assuming P \neq NP \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Checking consistency of two OBDDs with possibly different variable orders is NP-complete (Lemma~8.14 of \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}). Since every OBDD is an \langref{nFBDD} and consistency for \langref{nFBDD} is polynomial-time decidable, a polynomial-time $\land$BC transformation for \langref{nFBDD} would imply P = NP.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_d24efe0e, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{nOBDD} supports Bounded Conjunction not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) assuming P \neq NP \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Checking consistency of two OBDDs with possibly different variable orders is NP-complete (Lemma~8.14 of \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}). Since every OBDD is an \langref{nOBDD} and consistency for \langref{nOBDD} is polynomial-time decidable, a polynomial-time $\land$BC transformation for \langref{nOBDD} would imply P = NP.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_4e62a038, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{uFBDD} supports Bounded Conjunction not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) assuming P \neq NP \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Checking consistency of two OBDDs with possibly different variable orders is NP-complete (Lemma~8.14 of \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}). Since every OBDD is a \langref{uFBDD} and consistency for \langref{uFBDD} is polynomial-time decidable, a polynomial-time $\land$BC transformation for \langref{uFBDD} would imply P = NP.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_c2df8c2b, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langref{uOBDD} supports Bounded Conjunction not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) assuming P \neq NP \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Checking consistency of two OBDDs with possibly different variable orders is NP-complete (Lemma~8.14 of \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998}). Since every OBDD is a \langref{uOBDD} and consistency for \langref{uOBDD} is polynomial-time decidable, a polynomial-time $\land$BC transformation for \langref{uOBDD} would imply P = NP.
\end{claimdescription}


% =============================
\section{R. E. Bryant, "Graph-based algorithms for boolean function manipulation", IEEE T...}
% Reference ID: Bryant_1986
% =============================
% lang=lang_d69995dd, op=NOT_C
\begin{claim}
\langfam{OBDD}{<} supports Negation in polynomial time \citet{Bryant_1986}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Negation of an OBDD$_<$ is achieved by switching the labels of the 0-sink and 1-sink. The result is an OBDD$_<$ of identical size \citet{Bryant_1986}.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_d69995dd, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langfam{OBDD}{<} supports Bounded Conjunction in polynomial time \citet{Bryant_1986}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
The Apply algorithm computes the conjunction of two OBDD$_<$ sentences sharing the same variable ordering in polynomial time. The result is a reduced OBDD$_<$ \citet{Bryant_1986}.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_d69995dd, op=OR_BC
\begin{claim}
\langfam{OBDD}{<} supports Bounded Disjunction in polynomial time \citet{Bryant_1986}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
The Apply algorithm computes the disjunction of two OBDD$_<$ sentences sharing the same variable ordering in polynomial time. The result is a reduced OBDD$_<$ \citet{Bryant_1986}.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_d69995dd, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langfam{OBDD}{<} supports Conditioning in polynomial time \citet{Bryant_1986}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Conditioning (the restrict operation) on OBDD$_<$ replaces each node labeled by a variable in the conditioning term with one of its children, according to the sign of the variable. The result is a valid OBDD$_<$ \citet{Bryant_1986}.
\end{claimdescription}


% =============================
\section{G. Van den Broeck and A. Darwiche, "On the Role of Canonicity in Knowledge Compi...}
% Reference ID: VanDenBroeck_2015
% =============================
% lang=lang_82fa749e, op=NOT_C
\begin{claim}
\langfam{cSDD}{T} supports Negation in polynomial time \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
\citet{VanDenBroeck_2015} proved that reduced \langfam{SDD}{T} formulas support negation in polynomial time, by passing negation through the subs.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_82fa749e, op=AND_BC
\begin{claim}
\langfam{cSDD}{T} supports Bounded Conjunction not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
\citet{VanDenBroeck_2015} proved that combining two \langfam{cSDD}{T} formulas can force exponential \langfam{cSDD}{T} size.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_82fa749e, op=CD
\begin{claim}
\langfam{cSDD}{T} supports Conditioning not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
\citet{VanDenBroeck_2015} proved that conditioning a \langfam{cSDD}{T} formula on a literal can force exponential \langfam{cSDD}{T} size.
\end{claimdescription}

% lang=lang_82fa749e, op=SFO
\begin{claim}
\langfam{cSDD}{T} supports Singleton Forgetting not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
\citet{VanDenBroeck_2015} proved that forgetting one variable from a \langfam{cSDD}{T} formula can force exponential \langfam{cSDD}{T} size.
\end{claimdescription}


% =============================
\section{H. Vinall-Smeeth, "Structured d-DNNF Is Not Closed Under Negation", Proceedings ...}
% Reference ID: Vinall-Smeeth_2024
% =============================
% lang=lang_ea9b5299, op=NOT_C
\begin{claim}
\langref{d-SDNNF} supports Negation not in polynomial time (quasi-polynomial unknown) \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
Structured deterministic DNNF (\langref{d-SDNNF}) is not closed under negation in polytime. \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} proves that for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$, there exists a Boolean function $f$ with an equivalent structured $d$-DNNF of size $n$ such that any structured DNNF equivalent to $\neg f$ has size $n^{\Omega(\tilde{\log}\, n)}$. This super-polynomial lower bound holds for every vtree (not just a single tree), so the result applies to \langref{d-SDNNF} (not just \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}). Whether quasi-polynomial closure holds remains open.
\end{claimdescription}


% =============================
\section{J. Lang and P. Liberatore and P. Marquis, "Propositional Independence: Formula-V...}
% Reference ID: Lang_2003
% =============================
% lang=lang_4c204bf3, op=FO
\begin{claim}
\langref{DNF} supports Forgetting in polynomial time \citet{Lang_2003}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
DNF satisfies FO \citet{Lang_2003}. Forgetting variables from a DNF sentence can be done in polytime (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}


% =============================
\section{A. Darwiche, "Decomposable Negation Normal Form", Journal of the ACM, vol. 48, p...}
% Reference ID: Darwiche_2001a
% =============================
% lang=lang_3bebcab7, op=FO
\begin{claim}
\langref{DNNF} supports Forgetting in polynomial time \citet{Darwiche_2001a}
\end{claim}
\begin{claimdescription}
DNNF satisfies FO: forgetting a set of variables in a DNNF sentence can be performed in polynomial time while preserving decomposability \citet{Darwiche_2001a} (\citet{Darwiche_2002}, Proposition 5.1).
\end{claimdescription}
